title: Bank Capital and Lending Relationships
abstract: This paper investigates the mechanisms behind the matching of banks and firms in the loan market and the implications of this matching for lending relationships, bank capital, and credit provision. I find that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks, while firms with access to the bond market borrow from banks with less capital. This matching of bank-dependent firms with stable banks smooths cyclicality in aggregate credit provision and mitigates the effects of bank shocks on the real economy.

article.introduction:
Banking relationships are an important sourceof external finance for many firms. An extensive literature highlights the effects of banking relationships on firms' cost of capital and nonfinancial outcomes.1An important segment of this literature shows that informational frictions make it costly for firms to switch lenders, leading to a “credit channel” that transmits shocks from banks to their borrowers.2While the effects of banking relationships are well understood, little evidence exists on the determinants of these relationships. What drives a firm to borrow from one bank instead of another? Do banks specialize in lending to certain types of borrowers? And if endogenous matching occurs in the loan market, what are the consequences for credit provision? I address these questions in this paper.
I begin by showing that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks, while firms with access to the public debt markets borrow from banks with less equity capital. Firms without a public debt rating, which I define as bank-dependent, borrow from banks that have 1.8% higher equity capital than banks that lend to rated firms. This difference is economically significant, equivalent to 14% of the mean and 27% of the standard deviation of the bank capital ratio. This sorting is evident nearly every year between 1987 and 2012 and is robust to the empirical approach used to identify it. My paper is the first to uncover this robust stylized fact on the matching of banks and firms.
This endogenous matching has important implications for the effects of bank shocks on the real economy and the overall cyclicality of credit provision. Informational frictions prevent bank-dependent borrowers from offsetting reductions in loan supply from their relationship banks. Bank health is closely related to lending activity, with well-capitalized banks better able to absorb shocks.3Figure1presents a concrete example, from the 2008 financial crisis, of the association between bank capital and lending activity following a macroeconomic shock. Consider two salient points on this plot, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Wells Fargo (WFC). JPMorgan Chase had a market equity ratio of 11.9% prior to the crisis and cut lending by 75% during the crisis, while Wells Fargo had a market equity ratio of 21.6% and cut lending by 48%. This anecdotal evidence suggests that obtaining credit from Wells Fargo would have been easier than obtaining credit from JPMorgan Chase during the financial crisis.
The intuitive takeaway from this plot is that bank-dependent firms benefit from having relationships with better capitalized banks, like Wells Fargo, that cut lending less in a downturn.4Based on a counterfactual exercise from my empirical matching model, I find that, in the period surrounding the financial crisis, bank-dependent firms faced 6.6% less loan supply shrinkage from their precrisis relationship banks relative to the reverse matching assignment, that is, bank-dependent firms borrowing from low-capital banks. This represents a significant improvement in these firms' position in the cross section of banks, equivalent to 42% of the standard deviation of bank lending growth during the crisis. The implication of this finding is that matching mitigates the impact of financial sector shocks on the real economy. Prior findings on the effects of bank shocks on firms' ability to invest in production (Gertler and Gilchrist (1994)) and employment (Chodorow-Reich (2014)) would be even stronger if banks were homogeneous or the matching of firms and banks were random.
The matching of bank-dependent firms with well-capitalized banks is also beneficial in the aggregate because firms with access to public debt markets can partially offset reductions in loan supply by issuing bonds. Figure2shows that the borrowers of poorly capitalized banks were substantially more likely to issue bonds in the period immediately following the financial crisis. This result extends Becker and Ivashina (2014), who find evidence of aggregate substitution from loans to bonds during this period, by showing that such substitution concentrated among borrowers of banks that reduced lending more during the crisis.5If the matching of firms and banks were such that more firms with access to public debt markets had relationships with well-capitalized banks, then there would be less substitution by these firms while bank-dependent firms would have less access to bank credit. Therefore, endogenous matching smooths the overall cyclicality of credit provision by amplifying the aggregate substitution from loans to bonds in a downturn.
I arrive at the above findings by taking various empirical approaches to a data set of syndicated loans merged with borrower and lender characteristics. First, I conduct regression analysis to uncover the key correlations between firm and bank characteristics, to narrow the mechanisms behind the matching of banks and firms, and to explore the pricing consequences of this matching. I find that both cross-sectional and within-bank time-series variations are consistent with well-capitalized banks specializing in lending to bank-dependent firms.
Next, to obtain a more structured characterization of the matching equilibrium, I estimate the Fox (2016) semiparametric matching model. This model allows me to quantify trade-offs across match characteristics and to generate counterfactual matching assignments, which I use to derive quantitative implications of matching for credit provision. I employ the Fox (2016) model rather than alternative matching models (e.g., Sorensen (2007)) or models of differentiated product demand (e.g., Berry, Levinsohn, and Pakes (2004)) because it allows agents to split the match surplus with transfer payments, but treats these transfers as unobservable, which avoids the need for counterfactual loan spreads and valuation of nonprice contract terms. Chen and Song (2013) also use the Fox (2016) model to study the syndicated loan market. They find that firms and banks match by size, but they do not explore matching by bank capital structure.
I derive hypotheses on the matching equilibrium using mechanisms from the banking theory literature. Theories of relationship lending (Sharpe (1990), Rajan (1992)), bank monitoring (Holmstrom and Tirole (1997), Coval and Thakor (2005), Allen, Carletti, and Marquez (2011), Mehran and Thakor (2011)), and bank risk management (Gornall and Strebulaev (2015)) predict that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks. However, theories in which bank fragility induces monitoring (Calomiris and Kahn (1991)) and liquidity creation (Diamond and Rajan (2001)) as well as theories of moral hazard and risk-shifting (Admati et al. (2013)) predict the opposite sorting, that is, that bank-dependent firms borrow from poorly capitalized banks. Ultimately, how firms and banks choose each other is an empirical question that I address in this paper.
To narrow the mechanisms that could explain the matching of bank-dependent firms with well-capitalized banks, I exploit the different roles of lenders in the syndicate. The evidence is consistent with relationship lending and monitoring, but inconsistent with risk management, as determinants of the observed matching. Bank-dependent firms match with well-capitalized lead arrangers, who monitor the borrower, but there is no sorting by capital to participant lenders, who bear default risk but do not monitor.6
Regarding interpretation of my results, a word of caution is in order. Note that while I characterize the two-sided matching of banks and firms, I cannot distinguish which party initiates the negotiation and I do not make claims about the split of the surplus. In describing the matching, I make statements like “firms borrow from banks” or “banks lend to firms.” Such statements are intended to be descriptive only, that is, they are not intended to indicate the relative negotiating power or profits of individual agents.
This paper contributes to several strands of literature. First, it adds to the extensive literature on relationship lending by improving our understanding of the determinants of these relationships. Prior literature shows that the matching of firms and banks depends on size (Stein (2002), Hubbard, Kuttner, and Palia (2002), Cole, Goldberg, and White (2004), Berger et al. (2005)), geographic proximity (Petersen and Rajan (1995,2002)), export country specialization (Paravisini, Rappoport, and Schnabl (2016)), and the preexisting relationships of the firm (Bharath et al. (2007)) and its executives (Engelberg, Gao, and Parsons (2012), Karolyi (2017)). Jing (2014) further shows that firms of low credit quality borrow from banks that specialize in monitoring, and Ongena and Smith (2001) and Gopalan, Udell, and Yerramilli (2011) show that small, high-growth firms are more likely to switch banks to obtain additional credit. I extend this literature by providing evidence on the role of bank capital structure and the operation of the credit channel. While prior literature on the credit channel focuses on the ex post effects of bank shocks on borrowers, I show that firms and banks naturally match in a way that mitigates these effects. Bolton et al. (2016) find evidence of a similar mechanism, showing that Italian banks that lend to local firms had higher capital ratios and cut lending less after the financial crisis. My paper extends this work by drawing a connection between bank capital and borrower characteristics, independent of bank size. I show that this matching improves aggregate credit provision during a crisis, as the borrowers of fragile banks are best equipped to offset reductions in bank lending.
This paper also contributes to the literature relating bank capital structure to banks' intermediation activities.7My paper is the first to directly tie the bank's capital structure to its lending clientele. Banks with more equity capital lend more to bank-dependent firms, which suggests that capital is effective at incentivizing monitoring effort. This sorting is stronger for pure lending banks without investment banking operations than it is for universal banks. The incentive effects of equity on monitoring are weaker for universal banks because these banks have more diversified portfolios and lend to larger firms with lower costs of relationship termination. The consequence of this finding is that changes in bank capital, for instance, due to regulation, should have more (less) of an effect on the lending behavior of pure lending banks (universal banks). In this vein, my work also relates to the literature on optimal bank capital structure and capital regulation.8However, I find that the regulatory Tier 1 capital ratio is unrelated to lending clientele and the stability of loan supply, which suggests that market-based measures should be included in regulatory capital requirements.
The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. SectionI.outlines theoretical predictions on the matching of firms and banks. SectionII.describes the data and discusses measurement issues. SectionIII.presents regression results on matching and loan pricing. SectionIV.presents evidence from a semiparametric matching model. SectionV.concludes.
article.I. Conceptual Framework:
The theoretical literature on banking and lending relationships offers few predictions on how firms and banks should match, but several theories offer mechanisms that can be used to derive predictions. Interestingly, different mechanisms lead to different predictions, making this a question that requires empirical resolution. In this section, I outline the theoretical predictions that motivate my empirical analysis. These predictions are not mutually exclusive, so when I distinguish among them in the data, I show which mechanism has a stronger effect but I do not isolate a specific channel.
Sharpe (1990) and Rajan (1992) present models in which firms are unable to transfer the information generated over the course of a lending relationship. If the bank fails or becomes distressed and refuses to roll over loans, its high-quality borrowers are unable to transmit information about their quality to other lenders, so they pay higher rates after switching banks. Informationally opaque borrowers benefit more from lending relationships and suffer more from relationship termination, so these firms prefer stable banks. Likewise, stable banks are better able to extract relationship rents by continuing to lend through downturns. Empirical evidence suggests that capital enhances bank stability (Cornett et al. (2011), Beltratti and Stulz (2012), Fahlenbrach, Prilmeier, and Stulz (2012), Berger and Bouwman (2013), Carlson, Shan, and Warusawitharana (2013), Demirguc-Kunt, Detragiache, and Merrouche (2013)). Thus, therelationship lending hypothesispredicts that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks.9
Several theories suggest that equity capital incentivizes bankers to screen and monitor borrowers. Capital gives a bank a stake in loan performance and mitigates the moral hazard that arises when the bank loans out borrowed funds (Holmstrom and Tirole (1997), Coval and Thakor (2005), Allen, Carletti, and Marquez (2011), Mehran and Thakor (2011)). Equity, as an information-sensitive claim, also induces shareholders to monitor bank management (Boot and Thakor (1993)). Allen, Carletti, and Marquez (2011) therefore predict that firms for which monitoring adds value prefer to borrow from banks with high capital. For instance, small, informationally opaque firms benefit from the certification provided by strong screening and monitoring. From the bank's perspective, monitoring increases the returns to lending to borrowers of low credit quality. Theequity monitoring hypothesisthus predicts that bank-dependent and low credit quality firms should borrow from well-capitalized banks.
Boot, Greenbaum, and Thakor (1993) show that financially strong banks are less likely to renege on loan commitments. Bank-dependent firms value the bank's ability to honor its loan commitments because it is costly for them to switch to other sources of capital. Ivashina and Scharfstein (2010) find that this is an empirically relevant concern, showing that borrowers of Lehman Brothers drew down their credit lines to ensure that they had access to those funds. Thefinancial commitment hypothesisthus predicts that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks.
Another line of work focuses on the bank's risk management problem. Gornall and Strebulaev (2015) model the joint capital structure decision of the bank and its borrowers. They predict that low-leverage banks lend to high-leverage borrowers, while high-leverage banks lend to low-leverage borrowers. Therisk management hypothesisthus predicts that well-capitalized banks lend to low-credit-quality firms.
In sum, theories of private information, bank incentives, and bank risk management, combined with empirical evidence on bank capital and stability, predict that small, informationally opaque, and less creditworthy borrowers match with well-capitalized banks. However, while the theoretical support for the sorting of bank-dependent firms to high-capital banks is compelling, alternative theories predict the opposite sorting.
One set of theories suggests that bank fragility incentivizes monitoring (Calomiris and Kahn (1991)) and facilitates liquidity creation (Diamond and Rajan (2001)). According to these theories, the threat of a run by depositors induces the bank to monitor and collect payments from borrowers. Thefragility monitoring hypothesisthus predicts that informationally opaque and low-credit-quality firms borrow from high-leverage banks, which have strong incentives to monitor, while more transparent and creditworthy firms borrow from low-leverage banks.10
An alternative argument based on shareholder-creditor conflict runs counter to the risk management hypothesis. Admati et al. (2013) argue that high leverage induces banks to invest in riskier assets to increase the likelihood of high shareholder and management payouts, while failing to internalize the increase in expected distress costs borne by creditors. Furlong and Keeley (1989) and Calem and Rob (1999) also predict that poorly capitalized banks engage in risk-shifting. Therisk-shifting hypothesispredicts that low-capital banks lend to less creditworthy borrowers than high-capital banks.
Under several hypotheses of firm-bank matching, banks offer benefits to borrowers that depend on capital. If there is imperfect competition among banks, then they should earn a premium for offering these benefits.
The equity monitoring, relationship lending, and financial commitment hypotheses predict that bank-dependent borrowers value the monitoring and stability of future funding offered by a high-capital bank. Under these hypotheses, borrowers should pay a premium to borrow from a high-capital bank, relative to what they would pay to borrow from a low-capital bank.
Alternatively, under the fragility monitoring hypothesis, low-capital banks offer monitoring benefits to bank-dependent borrowers and can charge a premium for this service. Consistent with this view, Hubbard, Kuttner, and Palia (2002) find that low-capital banks earn a loan spread premium on loans to small and opaque borrowers, although they attribute this pattern to low-capital banks having a higher cost of capital.
As is evident from the discussion above, the literature on banking and lending relationships leads to conflicting predictions about the matching of firms and banks and the consequences of this matching for loan pricing. It is therefore necessary to test these hypotheses using data from the loan market.
article.II. Data:
To test the hypotheses outlined in the previous section, I construct a sample of syndicated loans matched with firm and bank characteristics. Below, I describe the sample construction, I discuss the measurement of the economic variables referred to in the hypotheses, and I summarize the sample characteristics.
The principal sources of data for this paper are LPC DealScan and Compustat. I merge information on corporate loans from DealScan with borrower and lender characteristics in the quarter of loan origination from Compustat to construct a sample of firm-bank-loan observations from 1987 to 2012.
My starting point is the DealScan-Compustat Link from Chava and Roberts (2008).11This table matches loan facilities from DealScan with borrower identifiers in Compustat between 1983 and August 2012. I start my sample in 1987 because DealScan coverage is sparse before then. Following the literature, I exclude loans to financial companies (SIC between 6000 and 6999) from the sample.
While the Chava and Roberts (2008) link table allows me to merge the loan data with borrower characteristics, a similar table is not available for lenders. I create a lender link table by hand-matching DealScan lender names with Compustat GVKEYs for all lenders with at least 50 loans or at least $10 billion in loan volume in the DealScan-Compustat sample.12Several bank mergers occurred during the sample period. When a bank exits the Compustat sample, I identify the acquiring bank using information from news articles.13It is important to track mergers because the acquired bank sometimes appears in DealScan after it has become a subsidiary of the surviving bank.
This matching results in good coverage of the DealScan-Compustat sample. I match Compustat identifiers for the lead arranger on 87% of the loans and 97% of the loan volume in the DealScan-Compustat sample. The primary cause of lost observations is missing data on borrowers in Compustat. After merging data on borrower characteristics in the quarter of loan origination, the sample contains 55% of the loans and 66% of the loan volume in the DealScan-Compustat sample.
My empirical analysis examines the association between borrower attributes and bank capital structure. The lenders in the sample are heterogeneous in terms of accounting and regulatory treatment, nonlending activities, and liability structure. Accounting rules vary across countries, which significantly impacts the interpretation of bank capital and other accounting ratios.14Due to these differences, I restrict my analysis to banks and borrowers based in the United States. To ensure comparability among U.S. banks, I exclude banks that do not have deposits reported in Bank Compustat. I make an exception to include Citigroup, which is not in Bank Compustat but has a substantial deposit base. The deposits restriction excludes pure investment banks (e.g., Goldman Sachs) and financial companies (e.g., CIT Group). I exclude Bank of New York Mellon and State Street Bank because these banks are primarily custodian banks and are not focused on lending.15These restrictions result in only a small loss of observations. The final sample includes 50% of the loans and 60% of the loan volume in the DealScan-Compustat sample.
Most loans in DealScan are syndicated, with one or more lead arrangers and several participating lenders. I focus my analysis on the lead arranger(s) rather than syndicate participants because the lead arranger has an active role in originating the loan and monitoring the borrower, whereas participants are passive investors. Thus, when I refer to the firm's bank or lender, unless I specify it as a participant, I am referring to the lead arranger on the loan.
The observation level in my sample is the firm-bank-loan triplet, with firm and bank characteristics observed at the end of the quarter of loan origination. Each bank makes several loans per quarter, and firms sometimes obtain multiple loans in a quarter or receive a single loan from multiple lenders. I account for the former type of repetition, as well as any other sources of within-bank or within-time correlation in errors, by double clustering all regressions by bank and quarter.16
The hypothesis development in SectionI.refers to economic characteristics of borrowers and banks that require empirical proxies for my analysis. I define a borrower as bank-dependent if it does not have a long-term issuer rating from Standard and Poor's (S&P). This definition has been used in prior empirical work (Kashyap, Lamont, and Stein (1994), Chava and Purnanandam (2011)) and serves as a measure of both the borrower's informational opacity and access to public debt markets. The main results are similar if I use borrower size instead of nonrated status to measure bank dependence.17To measure borrower default risk, I use the naive distance-to-default of Bharath and Shumway (2008), which performs similarly to the Merton (1974) distance-to-default in predicting default but does not require numerical estimation of asset volatility and the value of assets.18
Little consensus exists in the empirical literature on how to measure bank capital.19I measure bank capital using the ratio of market capitalization to quasi-market assets20at the bank holding company level, for several reasons. The use of the market value of equity rather than the book value is motivated by theory. Market capitalization measures shareholders' stake in the bank and the price at which the bank could raise new capital. In contrast, book equity is based on the past performance and financing activities of the bank, and depends on the timing of loan loss recognition and other accounting decisions. The mechanisms behind the hypotheses in this paper depend on the bank having a stake in its performance, the franchise value of the bank, and the information sensitivity of the bank's equity. Thus, all of these theories relate more closely to the market value than to the book value of equity.21
The decision to use an equity ratio instead of a risk-adjusted capital ratio is motivated by both theoretical and empirical concerns. In principle, the Tier 1 regulatory capital ratio, which weights assets by risk categories to facilitate comparison across banks with different portfolios, is useful as a measure of bank safety. However, the numerator of Tier 1 capital has similar shortcomings as the book equity ratio, and the denominator is informative only if the risk-weighting classification accurately captures asset risk. In the Internet Appendix, I show that Tier 1 capital performs significantly worse than market equity at predicting changes in bank loan volume around the 2008 financial crisis. In a regression of lending growth around the financial crisis on precrisis capital, theR2for market equity is 54% and thet-statistic is 4.20, whereas theR2for Tier 1 capital is 14% and thet-statistic is 1.51. Excluding the high Tier 1 capital outliers—Northern Trust and PNC Bank—reduces theR2to 3% and thet-statistic to 0.62.22
Most of the lenders in my sample are subsidiaries of large bank holding companies. I measure capital at the bank holding company level, rather than at the banking subsidiary level. Houston, James, and Marcus (1997) show that loan growth at banking subsidiaries is sensitive to the holding company's capital position and insensitive to the subsidiary's own capital, due to the operation of internal capital markets. Additionally, the “source of strength” doctrine of the Federal Reserve induces bank holding companies to prevent their banking subsidiaries from failing, so the financial strength of the holding company directly affects the financial strength of its subsidiaries (Ashcraft (2008)).
TableIreports summary statistics for my sample. All variables are winsorized at the 1% level to mitigate the influence of outliers. Loan characteristics are presented at the firm-bank-loan level, firm characteristics are presented by firm-quarter, and bank characteristics are presented by bank-quarter.
The sample includes 32,141 firm-bank-loan observations and 26,833 distinct loans. The median loan in the sample is a $180-million revolving credit facility with four-year maturity and a credit spread of 175 basis points. The bank retains 20% of the typical loan, for an investment of 0.14% of its book equity. Most loans are for general corporate purposes and working capital. About three-quarters of the loans are revolving credit facilities and about one-quarter are term loans.
The sample includes 21,710 firm-quarter observations for 4,712 firms. The typical sample firm is large and well established, having higher leverage and profitability than the average firm in Compustat. These firms are less likely than the typical Compustat firm to be subject to the information asymmetry and financial constraints behind many of the hypotheses of firm-bank matching. Nevertheless, the literature suggests that the bank-dependent firms in the sample are affected by financial frictions. Lin and Paravisini (2011) and Chodorow-Reich (2014) show that small, nonrated borrowers in the DealScan-Compustat sample suffered from reductions in bank lending around the WorldCom bankruptcy in 2002 and the Lehman Brothers bankruptcy in 2008, respectively.23Moreover, a large proportion of the firms in my sample have neither a public debt rating nor bonds outstanding, which suggests that many of them do not have access to public debt markets. In 43% of firm-quarter observations, the firm does not have a long-term issuer rating from S&P, while in 60% of observations, the firm does not have bonds outstanding.
The banks in the sample are large, with the median bank having $49 billion in assets. This result is expected, as the sample focuses on lead arrangers in the syndicated loan market. The bank market equity ratio exhibits substantial variation, with a mean of 12.85% and a standard deviation of 6.53%. Book equity ratios are lower and less variable, with a mean and standard deviation of 7.47% and 1.96%.
TableIIreports statistics on lending activity by the 33 banks that served as lead arranger for more than 50 loans in the sample. In total, 59 banks served as lead arranger on at least one loan in the sample. As mentioned before, several bank mergers occurred in the sample period. TableIIuses the names and identifiers of the surviving bank holding companies in Compustat as of September 2015.24Lending activity is summarized by the fraction of loans and the fraction of loan volume in the sample, split by the bank's role as lead arranger or participant. The top five lead arrangers, JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, Citigroup, Wells Fargo, and Wachovia, account for 65% of loans and 80% of loan volume in the sample. The dominance of these “universal” banks is unsurprising, as they are the largest commercial banks in the United States during the sample period. All results in this paper are robust to excluding universal banks from the sample and considering only pure lending banks without investment banking arms. TableIIalso reports statistics on syndicate participation by the most active lead arrangers. The market for syndicate participants is less concentrated than the market for lead arrangers, with 16 banks participating in at least 5% of loans in the sample.25Interestingly, a number of banks are relatively inactive as lead arrangers but quite active as participants in syndicated loans.
article.III. Regression Analysis:
This section presents an empirical analysis of the hypotheses presented in SectionI.. First, I present evidence that bank-dependent firms tend to borrow from well-capitalized banks. Next, I use different lender roles and loan types to narrow down the mechanisms driving this matching. Finally, I show that high-capital banks earn a loan spread premium when lending to small firms.
In all regressions, I include calendar quarter fixed effects to remove time trends. I control for bank size to distinguish the effects of size and capital, as larger banks tend to have lower capital ratios. I further control for borrower characteristics (asset tangibility, profitability, cash, operating leverage, Tobin's Q, years since IPO, and industry and state dummies) and loan characteristics (maturity, type, and purpose) to mitigate the impact of omitted factors that are correlated with both the borrower attributes of interest and the choice of bank. Finally, some specifications include bank or firm fixed effects, which remove time-invariant factors that drive matching.
TableIIIreports the regression estimates. The coefficient on the bank-dependent indicator is positive and statistically significant, indicating that banks lending to nonrated firms have higher equity capital ratios than banks lending to rated firms. Column (1) of Panel A implies that bank-dependent firms borrow from banks that have 1.78% higher capital, which is equivalent to 14% of the mean bank capital ratio and 27% of the standard deviation. Controlling for bank size reduces the coefficient by about half because size is negatively correlated with capital. Bank capital ratios are uncorrelated with the distance-to-default of borrowers. This result supports the hypotheses that emphasize information costs and access to outside funding but does not support the risk management hypothesis, which emphasizes credit risk.26In the Internet Appendix, I show that all of the control variables in column (3) have insignificant coefficients.
The relation between borrower bank-dependence and bank capital remains significant after including borrower fixed effects. The interpretation is that borrowers switch from a high-capital bank to a low-capital bank after acquiring a credit rating, or switch from a low-capital bank to a high-capital bank after losing a credit rating.27The magnitude of the coefficient decreases by about half relative to column (3), due to most borrowers either having a rating or not having a rating for the entire sample period. Interestingly, theR2increases from 67% to 81% after the addition of borrower fixed effects, despite all of the observable borrower characteristics in the set of control variables being insignificantly related to bank capital. This suggests that there is an important unobservable element in the formation of lending relationships, as the borrower's identity adds substantial power above its observable characteristics in predicting the capital ratio of its relationship bank.
The addition of bank fixed effects reduces the coefficient on the bank-dependent indicator to statistical insignificance, with a magnitude one-fifth the size of the coefficient in column (3). This result could arise from lack of variation in bank capital or from time-invariant bank types driving the matching. In the next section, I explore this issue further by examining the connection between within-bank variation in capital structure and lending clientele.
Panel B of TableIIIconsiders only the pure lending banks without investment banking operations. This reduces the sample size dramatically, due to the dominance of the universal banks, but the results become stronger. In this subsample, bank-dependent firms borrow from banks that have 2.11% higher capital than banks that lend to rated firms. This difference remains economically significant at 1.39% after controlling for bank size. These results suggest that bank size, or differences in bank type between universal and pure lending banks, are not behind the finding that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks.28
The Internet Appendix contains results of several robustness tests in which I explore other subsamples of the data. The sorting of bank-dependent firms to high-capital banks occurs in almost every year of the sample period. The results are similar when the sample is restricted to single-lender loans, where the lead arranger retains the entire loan on its balance sheet, and hence are not driven by the syndication process. The results are also similar when the sample is restricted to the first loan between the firm and the bank, suggesting that relationship persistence does not drive the results. Finally, the matching results do not hold if risk-weighted Tier 1 capital is used to measure bank capital, likely for the same reasons Tier 1 capital is a poor predictor of crisis performance and lending activity.29This suggests that the willingness of the market to provide capital is more important than compliance with capital regulation for a bank's borrowing base.
Taken together, the results in TableIIIsupport the hypotheses predicting that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks, and are inconsistent with the fragility monitoring and risk-shifting hypotheses, which predict the opposite sorting. Next, I use within-bank variation in capital structure to examine whether the results in TableIIIreflect a direct association between bank capital and lending clientele or are instead driven by time-invariant bank types.
The coefficient of interest in TableIIIis reduced substantially by the inclusion of bank fixed effects, suggesting that it is driven primarily by cross-sectional variation. This raises the possibility that an omitted, time-invariant factor correlated with capital is behind the finding that high-capital banks specialize in lending to bank-dependent firms. In this section, I attempt to rule out this possibility by focusing on within-bank time-series variation that cannot be driven by time-invariant factors. Specifically, I examine how the lending clientele of each bank is associated with its capital ratio relative to other banks.
If capital matters for lending to bank-dependent firms, as predicted by the hypotheses in SectionI., then the evolution of a bank's capital structure and the evolution of its lending clientele should be closely related. However, relationship frictions can weaken this association. If the bank experiences a shock to its capital and recovers quickly, then costs of relationship termination should prevent the borrowing base from switching banks. On the other hand, if the bank permanently changes its capital ratio relative to other banks, then its borrower base should eventually adjust to match the new capital structure.30The results in column (5) of TableIIIsuggest that within-bank changes in capital and lending clientele are statistically unrelated, but this confounds banks with small, transitory changes in capital and banks with large, permanent changes in capital. The former group is not expected to experience changes in lending clientele due to relationship frictions, while the latter group is expected to adjust capital and lending clientele jointly.31
TableIVpresents evidence on within-bank variation in capital and its relation to lending clientele. I measure relative capital using the cross-sectional decile of each bank's capital ratio. Using a relative measure of capital rather than an absolute level removes market-wide trends in a manner analogous to the time fixed effects in TableIII. The first two columns examine time series variation in relative bank capital. Banks are split into high-range and low-range groups by the total amount of variation in their capital decile over the sample period. High-range banks experience a range of more than five deciles between their minimum and maximum capital deciles. Most banks experience wide variation in capital over the sample period: 22 of the 33 banks that served as lead arranger on at least 50 loans satisfy the high-range criterion. Banks are then sorted in descending order by the absolute value of their time trend coefficient, which is the average amount the bank's capital decile changes from quarter to quarter. Banks with strong time trends experience long-term changes in relative capital, whereas banks with weak time trends experience transitory changes in relative capital.
I test my predictions on the joint evolution of capital and lending clientele by regressing the proportion of loans to bank-dependent borrowers on the relative capital of each bank in the time series. One confounding factor is bank size. If the bank grows over time relative to other banks, then it is likely to lend to fewer bank-dependent firms and its relative capital ratio is likely to decrease due to diversification and other factors that drive the negative correlation between size and capital. To account for this, I control for relative bank size in the second set of within-bank regressions.32
The regression coefficients on the right-hand side of the table indicate a strong association between relative capital and lending clientele for high-range banks with strong time trends. Seven of the 15 high-range banks with an absolute trend exceeding 0.03 have a positive and significant correlation (at the 1% level) between the proportion of loans to bank-dependent firms and capital decile in the time series.33Five of these banks decreased and two increased their capital decile over time. These results are striking, given the limited statistical power allowed by regressions estimated at the bank-quarter level. The number of positive and significant coefficients in the top 15 banks in the table is 47 times the number expected if the effects were random.34Consistent with relationship frictions preventing small, short-term changes in capital from driving changes in lending clientele, only one of the low-range banks shows a weakly significant correlation between relative capital and lending clientele.
The results in TableIVshow that capital structure and lending clientele are closely related as the bank evolves over time. This evidence sheds light on the insignificant coefficient in column (5) of TableIIIand supports the notion that capital structure itself, rather than an omitted time-invariant factor, is behind the finding that well-capitalized banks match with bank-dependent firms. In SectionI., I offer several mechanisms that predict this sorting. Next, I attempt to narrow these mechanisms to better understand why firms and banks match in this way.
In this section, I attempt to distinguish among the hypotheses predicting that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks: relationship lending, equity monitoring, financial commitment, and risk management. First, I use the different roles of syndicated loan lead arrangers and participants to distinguish between informational hypotheses and hypotheses relating to credit risk and commitment. Second, I use the difference between revolving credit facilities and term loans to test whether financial commitment drives the firm-bank matching.
Thus far, my analysis has focused on lead arrangers because the lead arranger most closely represents a relationship lender in banking theory. The lead arranger communicates directly with the borrower, screens its credit quality, negotiates loan terms, and monitors for covenant violations. To reduce its exposure to the borrower, the lead arranger recruits passive participant lenders to fund part of the loan.35This distinction between the roles of different syndicate members allows for more refined hypothesis testing.
The theoretical predictions for participant lenders are slightly different from the predictions for lead arrangers. In particular, the relationship lending and equity monitoring hypotheses do not apply to participants because participants do not communicate directly with the borrower, and thus are not expected to possess soft information. However, the risk management and financial commitment hypotheses apply to participants in the same way as lead arrangers because a participant is exposed to default risk and can renege on its share of an undrawn loan commitment.
TableVreports estimates of the regression in equation1, after including both lead arrangers and participant lenders in the sample.36The bank-dependent borrower indicator and distance-to-default are interacted with an indicator for whether the bank is a lead arranger, which allows for the sorting of firms to lead arrangers and participants. In the full sample, the interaction coefficients in the top row are similar in magnitude to the coefficients in TableIIIand marginally statistically significant, reflecting the sorting of bank-dependent borrowers to well-capitalized lead arrangers. These coefficients are highly significant when the sample is restricted to pure lending banks without investment banking operations. In contrast, the coefficients on the bank-dependent borrower indicator in the second row are statistically indistinguishable from zero, implying no association between bank-dependence and the capital of participant lenders.
The findings in TableVhighlight the different roles of lead arrangers and participants in the loan syndicate. While capital is an important trait for matching with the lead arranger, it appears that borrowers do not condition on the capital ratios of participants. Under the relationship lending and equity monitoring hypotheses, the capital of participants is irrelevant if participants do not gather any private information when lending to the borrower. This is consistent with participants having an arms-length relationship with the borrower, but contrasts with Sufi's (2007) finding that participant relationships matter for opaque firms. An alternative explanation is that the borrower signs an agreement with the lead arranger before participants are chosen, so the borrower cannot condition on participant characteristics. However, if participant capital were important to borrower welfare, then borrowers would demand the opportunity to select participants on this dimension. The contrasting results for lead arrangers and participants support the relationship lending and equity monitoring hypotheses, which posit an important role for lead arrangers but not for participants.
The results in TableVare inconsistent with the risk management and financial commitment hypotheses of firm-bank matching, which apply to both lender roles. Under the risk management hypothesis, participants with high capital should specialize in lending to bank-dependent borrowers.37Under the financial commitment hypothesis, borrowers should prefer well-capitalized participants. The lack of any correlation in the data between participant capital and lending clientele casts doubt on these hypotheses. In conjunction with the lack of correlation between bank capital and borrower default risk in TableIII, it seems that informational frictions are more important than credit risk in driving the matching of banks and firms.
Another useful distinction in my data is the difference between revolving credit facilities and term loans. Revolving credit facilities make up nearly three-quarters of the loans in the sample and term loans comprise most of the remainder. Revolving facilities can be drawn and repaid several times over the life of the loan. In contrast, term loans are fully drawn at origination and can be prepaid before maturity, but cannot be redrawn after repayment.
Under the financial commitment hypothesis, firms with high switching costs worry that the bank will renege on its commitment to an undrawn facility, forcing them to find alternative funding. However, this hypothesis relates only to revolving facilities and not to term loans, as term loans have no commitment element. The financial commitment hypothesis thus predicts that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks for revolving credit facilities but do not have a preference over banks for term loans. To test this prediction, I reestimate the regression in equation1, interacting the bank-dependent indicator and distance-to-default with an indicator for whether the loan is a revolving credit facility, to allow for different coefficients between revolving facilities and term loans.38
TableVIpresents evidence inconsistent with the financial commitment hypothesis. Bank-dependent firms tend to borrow from high-capital banks for both revolving credit facilities and term loans. In fact, the negative interaction coefficient means that the sorting of bank-dependent firms to high-capital banks is more pronounced for term loans than for revolving credit facilities.39This result holds in both the full sample and the subsample of pure lending banks.
The most important determinant of loan spreads is the borrower's credit risk. I control for credit risk with distance-to-default, indicator variables for each credit rating category (i.e., AAA, AA+, etc.), and loan amount as a percentage of borrower assets. In addition to the set of controls used in TableIII, I add indicators for whether the borrower issued bonds or stock during the life of the loan to account for bundled underwriting (Drucker and Puri (2005)), the number of years since the first loan between the firm and the bank to control for hold-up or learning, and the bank's position in the loan as a percentage of its book equity. I include calendar quarter fixed effects to account for time variation in market conditions. Finally, I consider borrower and borrower-year fixed effects to control for unobserved time-invariant and time-specific borrower heterogeneity.
TableVIIpresents estimates of the bank capital premium. Surprisingly, there is not much evidence of a premium for high-capital banks when lending to bank-dependent firms. The coefficient on the interaction of bank capital and the bank-dependent indicator is positive, as expected, but it is statistically insignificant after controlling for borrower credit risk.42The premium estimates for pure lending banks in Panel B of TableVIIare actually negative in several specifications.
While the lack of a loan spread premium is surprising, it does not imply rejection of the relationship lending and equity monitoring hypotheses. The loan spread divides economic surplus between the firm and the bank, so if competition and bargaining power favor the firm, then there is no loan spread premium. Moreover, it is possible that well-capitalized banks capture surplus in ways that I do not observe in the DealScan data, such as nonprice contract terms or other transactions between the firm and the bank. In the next section, I take the division of surplus as unobserved and study which firm-bank matches generate the most total surplus.
article.IV. Semiparametric Matching Model:
For a more structured description of the matching equilibrium, I turn to the semiparametric matching estimator developed by Fox (2016). This empirical model serves two purposes for my application. First, it estimates trade-offs among several characteristics of the firm-bank match, whereas the regression approach only describes pairwise correlations in the data. Second, it can generate counterfactual matching assignments, which I use to quantify the impact of endogenous matching on the provision of credit in a crisis.
The identification problem that differentiates this setting from other choice problems is that data on prices, or transfer payments between the firm and the bank, are unavailable. I do not observe the interest rate a firm would pay if it borrowed from a different bank from the one observed, and I am unable to estimate it precisely enough to use as a model input. Additionally, it is difficult to place a dollar value on nonprice loan terms that can also transfer value. The Fox (2016) model accounts for transfer payments in the equilibrium condition but treats them as unobservable, which is advantageous relative to alternative models of matching and multinomial choice that require price data.
Fox (2016) is the first to empirically estimate a many-to-many matching game with transferable utility; he applies his model to the matching of car parts suppliers and automotive assemblers.43In the context of the syndicated loan market, Chen and Song (2013) also use the Fox (2016) model, but they do not study the relation between the bank's capital structure and its lending clientele.
Fox (2016) takes the observed matching assignment as the outcome to be explained and estimates a latent match value function that drives this outcome. The estimator is based on the equilibrium concept of pairwise stability, which means that no pair of agents find it beneficial to break their existing matches to match with each other.44The pairwise stability condition involves transfer payments between the firm and the bank, but as described above, I do not have data on these transfer payments. Fortunately, Fox (2016) provides a way to estimate the match value function without data on transfer payments.45
To illustrate how the Fox (2016) estimator works, consider two loans from my sample. In the second quarter of 2005, Berry Petroleum borrowed from Wells Fargo and Bio-Rad Laboratories borrowed from JPMorgan Chase. The model compares the total latent value of the observed matches to the total value of the swapped matches pairing Berry Petroleum with JPMorgan Chase and Bio-Rad Laboratories with Wells Fargo. Estimation of the match value function involves swapping every observed pair of firm-bank matches each quarter and maximizing the number of these comparisons that are satisfied. The estimated match value relates to the total surplus shared by the firm and the bank, rather than the split of surplus between them, which is reflected in the transfer payments.
The model does not consider counterfactuals in which both firms borrow from one bank or either firm borrows from a lender outside the sample because evaluating these scenarios requires data on pricing. In other words, the model uses a subset of all possible matching assignments in the loan market. Under this restriction, parameter estimates are consistent as long as more valuable matches are more likely to occur in the data.46
One consequence of this condition is that the value function is driven by characteristics of the match, rather than the individual characteristics of firms and banks. Each firm and each bank appear on both sides of the inequality, so any individual characteristics cancel out. I translate the matching hypotheses to the model by including products of demeaned bank and firm characteristics as covariates (Chen and Song (2013), Akkus, Cookson, and Hortascu (2016)). For instance, the product of the bank-dependent borrower indicator and bank capital enters the value function as the analog to the coefficient on the bank-dependent borrower indicator in TableIII. A positive coefficient on this covariate means more value is generated by matching bank-dependent borrowers with high-capital banks, whereas a negative coefficient means that more value is generated by matching bank-dependent borrowers with low-capital banks.
The Fox (2016) model relies on some identifying assumptions that merit discussion. First, the model conditions on the observed matching assignment being the optimal one, as there are generally multiple equilibria in a many-to-one matching market with transfers. Second, it assumes that the firm and bank attributes in the value function are unaffected by the matching assignment. This is reasonable because the firm's size and location are unlikely to be affected by its choice of bank and each loan is small enough relative to the bank's assets not to affect its capital structure. Third, the pairwise stability condition imposes a capacity constraint on banks, with each bank lending to the same number of firms under all counterfactuals. This is necessary to cancel out transfer payments in equation. Although a constraint on the bank's dollar loan volume would be more realistic, neither the model parameters nor the counterfactual estimates are materially impacted by this assumption.47
The computational cost of estimating the Fox (2016) model increases rapidly with the size of the parameter set. With this in mind, I select a set of covariates that are closely related to the hypotheses on firm-bank matching, but exclude most of the control variables from the regression analysis. All firm- and bank-specific variables are demeaned each quarter so that their interactions can be interpreted as covariances.
The covariate of interest in the match value function is the interaction of the bank-dependent borrower indicator and bank capital. Bank size is an important confounding factor, as larger banks tend to have lower capital ratios. To account for matching by size, I include the interaction of bank size and borrower size in the match value function. Based on prior evidence on the importance of geographic distance in banking, I also include the distance between borrower and bank headquarters as a covariate. To account for industry specialization, I include an indicator for whether the borrower is in the bank's top three Fama-French 30 industries by volume in a given quarter. Finally, to capture relationship persistence, I include an indicator for whether there is a prior loan in the sample between the firm and the bank.
The objective function consists of inequalities, so identification is only up to arbitrary order-preserving transformations of the parameters (Manski (1975)). To scale the other parameters in the match value function, I fix the coefficient on the prior-relationship indicator to +1,000 or −1,000 and choose the specification that satisfies a greater percentage of the pairwise stability inequalities. Unsurprisingly, the positive coefficient yields a better model fit, indicative of relationship persistence.
TableVIIIreports estimates of the matching model that are consistent with the results of the regression analysis. The positive coefficient on the interaction of the bank-dependent borrower indicator and bank capital means that more value is generated by matching bank-dependent firms with well-capitalized banks. Consistent with prior literature, firms and banks that are located closer together are more likely to match, there is positive assortative matching by size, and banks specialize in lending to certain industries.
The coefficient on the interaction of the bank-dependent borrower indicator and bank capital remains significant after the removal of each individual covariate. This coefficient increases when the interaction of borrower size and bank size is removed, due to the positive correlation between size matching and matching on bank capital. Interestingly, all of the coefficient estimates remain similar after the removal of the prior-relationship indicator. This suggests that any omitted variables are uncorrelated with the covariates included in the model, since the prior-relationship indicator subsumes all factors that drove firms and banks to match in previous quarters.
The fit of the model is very good, with over 90% of the pairwise stability inequalities satisfied by the estimated parameters. This is comparable to or better than the fit reported in other papers using the Fox (2016) model (e.g., Chen and Song (2013), Akkus, Cookson, and Hortascu (2016), Fox (2016)). Another way to measure model fit is to assign firms to banks according to the estimated value function and check how many of the observed matches are predicted by the model.51The bottom panel of TableVIIIreports results of this exercise. The model does significantly better than random assignment at predicting matches, with 53% of the true matches predicted by the model parameters. The rate of prediction is lower than the proportion of satisfied inequalities due to the potential for multiple equilibria in a many-to-one matching market with transfers. Model fit is substantially worse when the prior-relationship indicator is excluded, underscoring the importance of unobservable elements of existing relationships in predicting the matching assignment.
Interpreting the magnitudes of the parameter estimates is not straightforward because the match value is arbitrarily scaled and the counterfactuals involve pairwise comparisons. However, trade-offs among model inputs can be established with indifference conditions, and the distance between the bank and the firm is a natural benchmark. The model implies that a bank-dependent firm would be willing to travel 55 miles, or 7% of the mean firm-bank distance, to borrow from a bank with one standard deviation higher capital.52Likewise, a firm in the 25thpercentile of size would be willing to travel 278 miles, or 36% of the mean firm-bank distance, to borrow from a bank that is one standard deviation smaller. This implies that matching by size is about five times as important as matching by capital structure, which is unsurprising given the pervasiveness of size matching in economic settings.
Overall, the model estimates are indicative of an equilibrium in which the economic surplus shared by firms and banks is enhanced by matching bank-dependent firms with well-capitalized banks. In the next section, I explore the implications of this matching for the provision of credit in a crisis.
One implication of matching bank-dependent firms with well-capitalized banks is that bank-dependent firms are less likely to suffer constrained access to credit in a downturn. I quantify this effect in the 2008 financial crisis by altering the parameters of the matching model and reassigning firms to banks under various counterfactual scenarios, and then measuring the effect of the matching assignment on bank-dependent firms' access to credit.
I focus on bank-dependent firms because firms with access to public debt markets are able to offset reductions in lending by issuing bonds. Figure2shows that the borrowers of poorly capitalized banks, who are predominantly large and rated, were significantly more likely to issue bonds during the crisis period than the borrowers of well-capitalized banks.53Thus, increasing the allocation of bank credit to bank-dependent firms improves aggregate access to credit because firms that are not bank-dependent can substitute into the public debt markets.
I measure a firm's access to credit during the financial crisis using the growth in lending by its precrisis relationship bank. Intuitively, a contraction in the supply of loans at a firm's relationship bank affects the firm's access to credit because it is costly to switch banks. Since I am unable to measure loan supply directly, it is important to demonstrate that cross-sectional differences in observed lending growth are attributable to supply differences across banks, rather than to the reduction in aggregate demand that accompanied the crisis.
Specifically, I compute lending growth as the percentage change in annualized loan volume by each bank from the precrisis period (October 2004 to June 2007) to the crisis period (October 2008 to June 2009).54In TableIX, I test whether supply drives cross-sectional differences in lending growth using a regression in the style of Khwaja and Mian (2008). Among firms that received loans during the crisis, both observable and unobservable demand are uncorrelated with the total change in lending by precrisis relationship banks, as evidenced by the minimal impact of controlling for borrower characteristics and fixed effects on the regression coefficient relating bank-borrower lending growth to the bank's total lending growth. This supports the notion that a bank's growth in loan volume is a good measure of the bank's growth in loan supply during the crisis.
To establish precrisis relationships, I use each firm's most recent loan from the precrisis period. I reestimate the model on the full sample of loans in the precrisis period to obtain the parameters that drive matching prior to the crisis. The coefficients are qualitatively similar to those in TableVIII. Counterfactual matching assignments are generated using the procedure described in the previous section. For each counterfactual assignment, I calculate the average lending growth of the bank-dependent firms' precrisis relationship banks and subtract it from the average lending growth under the observed matching assignment. Confidence intervals for this difference are formed using the sampling distribution of model parameters combined with bootstrapping for assignments involving randomness.
TableXreports estimates of the effect of matching on the provision of credit around the financial crisis. The typical bank-dependent firm saw its precrisis relationship bank reduce lending by 66% during the crisis. Lending growth under the matching assignment predicted by the model is indistinguishable from the observed outcome, which confirms that the matching model captures the key forces driving firms' access to credit. As a simple starting point, randomly matching banks and firms would result in bank-dependent firms facing a 2.9% larger reduction in loan supply around the financial crisis than under the observed matching assignment. To put this in the context of the cross section of banks, this difference is equivalent to 18% of the standard deviation and 5% of the range of loan supply growth. Thus, endogenous matching results in significantly better access to credit for bank-dependent firms relative to a random benchmark.
As an alternative counterfactual, suppose that informational frictions prevent all but the largest and most transparent firms from borrowing from distant banks. Matching all firms to the nearest bank would lead bank-dependent firms to face a 2.2% larger contraction in loan supply than they actually saw during the financial crisis. If the largest quarter of firms are able to match by the model in TableVIIIwhile the remaining firms match to the nearest bank, the effect is similar, with the typical bank-dependent firm seeing 2.4% lower growth in lending by its precrisis relationship bank. The provision of credit appears to be better under proximity matching than it is under random assignment, but the scenarios are statistically indistinguishable, as evidenced by their overlapping confidence intervals.
Finally, the model allows me to test the effect of each individual matching channel on access to credit. Shutting off the interaction between the bank-dependent borrower indicator and bank capital causes the firm to face a 1.6% larger contraction in loan supply during the financial crisis. This difference is smaller than the difference under random assignment, likely due to the positive correlation between this channel and the size matching channel. Reversing this interaction, so bank-dependent firms borrow from poorly capitalized banks, leads to a 6.6% reduction in lending growth relative to the observed outcome. This difference is economically significant, equal to 42% of the standard deviation and 12% of the range of lending growth. The bank capital channel of matching is unique in improving credit provision, as shutting off the other individual parameters in the matching model does not lead to significant effects on access to credit.
To put the benefits of endogenous matching in perspective, consider the effects of credit supply on employment by bank-dependent firms. Chodorow-Reich (2014) shows that borrowers of unhealthy banks reduced employment by more than borrowers of healthy banks during the financial crisis. My measure of lending growth closely mirrors Chodorow-Reich's (2014) measure of bank health, so I can use his results to construct a back-of-the-envelope estimate of how matching on bank capital affected employment growth during the crisis. I calculate that under the reverse matching, which pairs bank-dependent firms with poorly capitalized banks, firms without bond market access would have cut employment by 5% more than they did under the observed matching.55
These results show that the matching of bank-dependent firms with well-capitalized banks has a substantial impact on firms' access to credit in a banking crisis. Without this matching channel, bank-dependent firms would face larger cutbacks in lending by their relationship banks and would be forced to cut back on employment and real output. Overall, access to credit in a crisis is improved under this matching because bank-dependent firms have relationships with healthy banks, while firms with access to the public debt markets can issue bonds to partially offset reductions in lending by their less healthy relationship banks.
article.V. Conclusion:
In this paper, I present novel evidence on the mechanisms behind the matching of firms and banks and the implications of this matching for the provision of credit. I show that bank-dependent firms borrow from well-capitalized banks, while firms that can access the public debt markets borrow from banks with less capital. The evidence suggests that informational frictions and borrowers' access to outside funding are the factors driving this matching, rather than the risk management policy of banks.
With regard to external validity, this paper uses a sample of syndicated loans to public corporations, which are among the largest and most transparent firms in the U.S. economy. If data for smaller, private firms were available, I would expect them to sort more strongly to well-capitalized banks because these firms have higher costs of relationship termination and require stronger monitoring than the bank-dependent firms in my sample. However, this prediction depends on these firms having access to a local branch of a well-capitalized bank and other factors, such as management's personal banking relationships (Karolyi (2017)), not outweighing stability and monitoring incentives in the choice among banks.
My findings improve our current understanding of how financial sector shocks are transmitted to the real economy via the credit channel. Firms that are most susceptible to reductions in loan supply have relationships with the most resilient banks, while the borrowers of fragile banks are able to substitute into bonds when loan supply shrinks. This matching mitigates the effects of a banking crisis on firms' access to credit, relative to the alternative matching assignments discussed in the previous section.
In spite of this mitigating effect, prior literature finds that bank shocks have significant effects on the ability of firms to invest in production (Gertler and Gilchrist (1994)) and employment (Chodorow-Reich (2014)). This literature typically treats banks as ex ante homogeneous or implicitly assumes that the matching of banks and firms is as good as random. Either of these scenarios would lead to larger observed effects of bank shocks on the real economy. Bank-dependent firms would face worse access to financing under the relationship lending hypothesis and more severe information asymmetry under the equity monitoring hypothesis, relative to the observed matching of firms with heterogeneous banks.
The aggregate substitution between loans and bonds shown by Becker and Ivashina (2014) is amplified by this endogenous matching, as firms with access to public debt markets concentrate at less healthy banks that cut lending by more in a contraction. If the matching of firms and banks were such that more firms with access to public debt markets had relationships with strong banks, there would be less substitution by these firms, while bank-dependent firms would have less access to bank credit. Therefore, endogenous matching smooths the overall cyclicality of credit provision.
Additionally, these results shed light on heterogeneity among banks in how capital structure relates to lending activity. TableIIIshows that the association between capital and lending clientele is stronger for pure lending banks than for universal banks. However, in the Internet Appendix, I show that the relation between precrisis capital and lending growth during the financial crisis is similar for both types of banks. The relationship lending and equity monitoring hypotheses offer an explanation for these patterns. First, the borrowers of universal banks are larger on average, so they likely face lower costs of relationship termination. Second, the incentive effects of equity on monitoring effort are likely weaker for universal banks because they rely less on soft information (Stein (2002), Berger et al. (2005), Liberti and Mian (2009)) and have more diversified portfolios. Thus, the lending clientele of these banks is less sensitive to capital structure because the mechanisms behind the capital matching channel are weaker. One consequence of this result is that changes in bank capital structure, for instance, due to regulation, should be expected to have larger effects on the lending behavior of pure lending banks and less of an effect on universal banks.
Finally, my findings have implications for the changing environment in terms of regulation and technology. While I cannot speak to the welfare consequences of raising capital requirements, my results suggest that more capital improves the stability of loan supply and induces monitoring effort. Since before the financial crisis, banks have been increasingly relying on hard information, such as credit scores rather than soft information to make lending decisions (Petersen and Rajan (2002), Agarwal and Hauswald (2010)). This potentially explains why the matching of bank-dependent firms to high-capital banks becomes less pronounced in the second half of my sample period. If improvements in technology reduce the cost of gathering information about borrowers, banks may require less equity capital to exert monitoring effort. However, further research is necessary to understand whether greater production of hard information is a substitute for soft information when a bank needs to distinguish good-quality credits from bad-quality credits in a crisis episode.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
